August 2A, 1990
Ms. Jeanne Pri.tchard
Chief, Technicat Assistance
Branch Division
Fair Political Practices commission
428 J Street, suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 958 04 -08 07
Re: "Regu1atory Published Newsletters," Regulation Section
).8225 (b) (4) (C) ; Status as I'contributionrl
Dear Jeanne:
This firm serves as legal counsel to a cornrnittee calLed rrYes
on L26 - supporting the Alcohol Abuse and Drug Education Act of
1990, sponsored by Beer, Wine and Distilled Spirits Cornpanies.rr
This cornmittee has various supporters, including associations
which publish regular newsletters, which are in turn distributed
to the members of each association. The members of the
associations in some cases also publish regular newsletters
\^rhich are distributed to the customers of the respective
members. The committee wants to request the associations to
include in their regul-arly publ-ished newsletters an articfe
supporting Proposition 126 and recommend.inq that the
associations I nenbers include in their own respective
newsletters articLes urging their customers to support
Proposition 126.
Last week, I contacted the Technical Assistance Division by
telephone to confirm that the expenditures made for this purpose
would be exempt from reportability under the exception to the
definition of 'rexpenditure't contained in Regulation Section
L8225(b) (4) (C) . To my surprise, you subsequently informed peter
Bagatelos of this office that the 'tregularly published
newsletterrr exception created in the "expenditureri regulation
cited above did not appfy, and that all expenditures ior such
ne\,/sletter communications hrere reportable as non-nonetary
contributions. 
Because neither peter Bagatel-os nor I understand
the legaf analysis behind thj-s advice, I am writing to request
cl-ari fication.
As you know, the political 
Reform Act does not contain a
definition of the term rrnon-monetary contributionr or the term


Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 2a , l-99O
Page Two
'r in-kind contribution . rr llor^rever, the def inition of
"contributionrr contained in Government Code S82O15 states that
rrAn expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee or
elected officer is a contrj-bution to the candidate, comrnittee or
elected officer unless ful-I and adequate consideration is
received for naking the expenditure.rt (emphasis added) The
cited language essentially describes what is normally referred
to as a trnon-monetaryrt contribution, and necessarily requires an
examination of the term 'rexpenditure. rt The term rrexpenditurert
is defined in Government Code 582025 in part as a payment, a
forgiveness of a 1oan, a payment of a foan by a third party, or
an enforceable promise to make a paynent, unless it is clear
from the surrounding cj-rcumstances that it is not made for
political 
purposes . . .rt
?he term 'rexpenditure" i.s further defined in Regulation
L8225 (2 California Code of Regulations 518225). Subdivision
(b) of this section states that an expenditure trincludes any
monetary or non-monetary payment made by any person. 
that is
used for cornmunications which expressfy advocate the nomination,
election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or
candidates, or the qualification. 
passage or defeat of a clearly
identified ballot measure. rl
However, subdivision (4) of this section statess
"Notwithstandj-ng the provisions of this subsection, the term
expenditure does not include costs j"ncurred for
cornrnunicat ions which expressly advocate the nornination,
election or defeat of a clearLy identified candidate or
candidates or the qualification, 
passage or defeat of a
clearfy identified measure or neasures by. 
(C) A
regularly published ner,/sfetter or regularly pubfished
periodical , other than those specified in paragraph
(b) (4) (A), whose circulation is lirnited to an organizationrs
members, employees, sharehofders, other affiliated
individuals and those r^rho request or purchase the
publication. 
This paragraph applies only to the costs
regularfy incurred in publishing and distributing the
nev/sletter or periodical. 
If additional costs are incurred
because the newsletter or periodical is issued on other than
its regular schedule, expanded in circulation, or
substantially al.tered i-n style, size or format, the
additional costs are expenditures. rl
In the opinion of this firm, expenditures for a rrregufarly
published newsletterrt which meets the description set forth in
Regulatj-on S18225(b) (4) (C) are not "expendituresr as that term
is defined either j-n the Regulation or in Governnent Code
S82025. Because such payments do not constitute rexpendituresrl


Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 28, 1990
Page Three
as that term is used in these sections, the payments also do not
constitute " contribut ions rr as defined in Governrnent Code
S82015. The pfain language of the definition of 'r contr j,butionrr
does not encompass such paynents because they are not
'rexpenditures. tl
Moreover, it has al-ways been our understanding that the
other two iterns which are exempted by Regulation S18225(b) (4)
from the term rrexpendituretr are also commonly considered to be
exempt from the term rr contribution. rr These are the exemptions
for rrA regular]y published newspaper, magazine or other
periodical of general circulation which routinely carries news,
articl-es and commentary of general interesti and rrA federatly
regulated broadcast outlet.'r 
(See, 2 California Code of
Regul-ations 18225(b) (4)(A) and (B)). If these two items do not
involve expenditures, when rnade at the behest of a candidate or
cornmittee, and therefore are afso not rrcontributions, rr v/e cannot
understand why the exemption would not also apply to Itregularly
published ne$/sfetters.rr The same analysis should be appl-icabl-e
to a1l three of those iterns, and $re cannot understand why
nerr/sl-etters woul-d be singled out for separate treatment.
Because it appears clear to us that expenditures for
regularly published newsletters do not generally qualify either
as rrexpend ituresrr or rr contribut ions , rr your telephone advice that
all payments for such newsletters are reportable confused us.
Therefore, we would appreciate v,/rj-tten clarification 
of the
following questions:
(1) Are expenditures for regufarfy published newsletters/
as the term is defined in Regulation Sl-8225(b) (4) (c)
reportable as Itcontributionsrr if made at the behest of
a candidate or ba11ot neasure committee?
(2) If so, what are the legaf anafysis and authorities
supporting this interpretation? and
(3) If not, does the newsletter described in the first
paragraph of this l-etter qualify as 'rregularly
pubfished newslettersrr? For purposes of this question,
please assume that:
(a) the newsletter is published on its reguLar
schedule and is not altered in terms of scope,
circulation or otherwise i and
(b) the newsletter is distributed to customers of the
association nenbers who have either requested that
they be pfaced on the newsl-etters mailing Iist, 
or
have been pfaced on the nailing fist because they


Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Auqust 28, \990
Page Four
have become regular customers of the association
member .
We would appreciate your expedited response to the foregoing
questions, in light of the fact that the Proposition 126
campaign is now fully underway and campaign reports must be
f il"ed no later than October 5 for the period ending September
30. In the event that you have any additional guesti,ons, or
require additional information, please do not hesitate to
contact either Peter Bagatelos or rnyself at this office,
S incerely ,
uflxT,#*,
Of counsel
